stories.headline: Avalanche sweep Kings in first round, advance to second round of NHL playoffs
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche completed a first-round sweep of the Los Angeles Kings with a 5-1 victory Sunday at Crypto.com Arena in Los Angeles, punching their ticket to the second round of the Stanley Cup playoffs. Nathan MacKinnon scored twice, including an empty-net goal, while Cale Makar was dominant throughout the series. It marks just the third time in franchise history that Colorado has swept an opponent in the opening round.

The win ends the NHL career of Kings captain Anze Kopitar, who retired after 20 seasons with Los Angeles. The Avalanche will now await the winner of the series between the Minnesota Wild and the Dallas Stars.

Colorado's depth and defensive structure were on full display throughout the four-game series. Goaltender Scott Wedgewood also contributed a standout performance in overtime of Game 2, finishing that game with 24 saves, including a stop on a penalty shot.

stories.headline: Nuggets trail Timberwolves 3-1 as Jokić fined $50,000 following Game 4 altercation
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets now face elimination after losing Game 4 to the Minnesota Timberwolves, falling behind 3-1 in the first-round series. The game ended in controversy when Jaden McDaniels made a meaningless layup with 2.1 seconds remaining, prompting Nikola Jokić to charge across the court and confront McDaniels near the Timberwolves' bench, leading to ejections for both Jokić and Minnesota's Julius Randle.

The NBA announced Monday that Jokić has been fined $50,000 for instigating the altercation. Despite the incident, Jokić finished with 24 points, 15 rebounds and nine assists in 41 minutes of the 112-96 loss. The Nuggets failed to reach 100 points in back-to-back games for the first time this season.

Game 5 will be held at Ball Arena in Denver, with the Nuggets needing a win to extend their season. Christian Braun acknowledged the team is in a "really bad spot" but expressed confidence, noting that Jokić and Jamal Murray have come back from 3-1 deficits before, most notably during the 2020 NBA bubble.

stories.headline: Rockies complete sweep of Mets in New York with pair of victories
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies swept the New York Mets at Citi Field on Sunday, winning both games of a doubleheader by scores of 3-1 and 3-0. Starting pitchers Michael Lorenzen, Jose Quintana and Chase Dollander combined to allow just two earned runs over 19 1/3 innings across the series, posting a 0.94 ERA.

Quintana earned his first win of the season in the doubleheader opener, while the Rockies' offense was paced by timely hitting throughout the series. Colorado had opened the series Friday with a 4-3 victory.

The sweep sent the Mets to their 14th loss in 16 games and gave the Rockies a morale-boosting road trip. The series results represent one of Colorado's stronger stretches of play in recent memory.

stories.headline: Union authorizes strike against Denver Processing, a JBS subsidiary, over alleged labor violations
stories.summary:
Workers at Denver Processing, a wholly owned subsidiary of JBS USA, voted 97% in favor of authorizing an unfair labor practice strike, the United Food and Commercial Workers Local 7 announced Friday. The approximately 300 union members at the Denver plant process meat products for Kroger-affiliated grocery chains including King Soopers and City Market in Colorado.

The union accused Denver Processing of retaliating against a bargaining committee member for attending negotiation sessions and of bad-faith bargaining, including stalling tactics and declaring a final offer before meaningful negotiations occurred. Contract talks began in December.

JBS spokeswoman Nikki Richardson said the company is disappointed in the union's decision, arguing it has bargained in good faith and offered meaningful wage increases and a one-time bonus. The strike authorization comes weeks after UFCW Local 7 reached a tentative agreement with JBS at its flagship Greeley beef processing plant following a three-week strike. The timing of any actual work stoppage will be determined at a later date.

stories.headline: Hunter missing near Mount Shavano found dead after more than a week-long search
stories.summary:
The body of Kaden Sites, a Colorado man who went missing while hunting near Mount Shavano in Chaffee County, was recovered Saturday by the Chaffee County Sheriff's Office, ending a search that lasted more than 10 days. The location of the discovery was near Tabeguache Creek in the high country southwest of Salida.

Details on the cause of death have not been released. The case had drawn significant search and rescue resources over the course of the nearly two-week effort.

Mount Shavano is a 14,229-foot peak in the Sawatch Range, and the rugged terrain presents significant challenges for search operations. The Chaffee County Sheriff's Office has not released further information on the circumstances surrounding Sites' disappearance.

stories.headline: Denver City Council to weigh scooter contract, airport legal fees, and harm reduction services this week
stories.summary:
The Denver City Council returns Monday with a 24-page agenda that includes a long-awaited three-year license agreement with Veo to operate the city's shared bike and scooter program, which has made three trips through the Transportation and Infrastructure Committee before reaching the full council. Also on the agenda is a $3.4 million contract with Jewish Family Service of Colorado to provide rapid rehousing for at least 55 homeless individuals or families living with intellectual or developmental disabilities.

Council members will also consider adding $200,000 and two years to a contract with Denver-based Kaplan Kirsch LLC for airport legal services. The firm specializes in airport law, FAA regulatory matters and aviation governance. Earlier this year, three of Mayor Mike Johnston's top political appointees were named in a lawsuit alleging retaliation for spotlighting possible FAA violations tied to the Park Hill Golf Course land swap and other airport-related decisions.

Additional items include a $239,000 contract extension with the Harm Reduction Action Center for syringe access and naloxone services, an $80,000 settlement payment involving the Denver Police Department, and a public hearing resolution on the Welton Corridor redevelopment plan in the Five Points neighborhood.

stories.headline: Colorado lawmakers advance bills targeting chatbot safety and age verification for minors online
stories.summary:
Two bills moving through the Colorado legislature seek to establish new online protections for young people. Senate Bill 51 would require users to enter their age when setting up a new device to restrict access to age-limited apps, using attestation rather than ID verification to protect privacy. House Bill 1263 would create regulations for AI-powered chatbots, requiring them to disclose they are not human, restrict sexually explicit content and provide crisis resources to users who express suicidal thoughts.

Both bills have cleared their first chamber but face opposition from advocates who argue the measures are too lenient on technology companies. Cynthia Montoya, a Thornton mother whose 13-year-old daughter died by suicide in 2023 after months of interaction with a chatbot, testified that the legislation falls short of necessary protections.

Governor Jared Polis has not committed to signing either bill, with his office saying he is "focused on protecting innovation while helping parents keep youth safe." Sponsor Rep. Sean Camacho, a Denver Democrat, acknowledged a "political reality" in crafting legislation that can pass in a tech-friendly environment.

stories.headline: Colorado law allowing school shooting victims to sue may leave staff unprotected, legal analysts say
stories.summary:
The Claire Davis School Safety Act, passed in Colorado after a 2013 shooting at Arapahoe High School, was intended to allow victims of school shootings to sue for damages, but the law may contain an unintended loophole that excludes school staff from its protections. Two deans who were shot and injured at Denver's East High School in 2023 are believed to be the first staff members to file lawsuits under the act.

Legal experts and advocates are now scrutinizing whether the law's language, crafted primarily with students in mind, provides sufficient protection for employees working in school buildings. The question has emerged as schools across Colorado continue to grapple with campus violence.

The East High School case could serve as a significant legal test of the statute's scope. Colorado lawmakers and victim advocates have been reviewing the legislation to determine whether an amendment may be needed to close the gap.

stories.headline: Outside-funded legislative fellows raise conflict-of-interest questions at Colorado Capitol
stories.summary:
A new report from the Colorado Accountability Project raises questions about outside organizations funding staff positions embedded within the Colorado legislature. One example cited is a left-of-center nonprofit affiliated with the Denver Museum of Nature and Science that funds "science fellows" to work alongside the nonpartisan legislative council staff.

Critics argue that having privately funded fellows within a supposedly nonpartisan legislative body creates a conflict of interest, potentially allowing outside groups with specific policy agendas to influence the legislative process without public oversight. The Colorado Accountability Project says the problem is not the fellows themselves but the precedent set by allowing private foundations to effectively staff public offices.

The report also touches on what it describes as a lack of ideological diversity in Colorado newsrooms and the unchecked influence of unelected regulatory bodies such as the Public Utilities Commission. The Independence Institute, a free-market think tank that publishes Complete Colorado, produced the report.

stories.headline: Evergreen High School shooting report released, detailing shooter's movements and law enforcement response
stories.summary:
A nearly 700-page report on the Evergreen High School shooting that occurred last September has been released, providing a detailed account of the shooter's actions throughout the day of the attack, the law enforcement response, and interviews with friends, family, teachers and witnesses. The report represents one of the most comprehensive official reviews of a Colorado school shooting in recent years.

The findings are expected to inform policy discussions about school safety protocols across the state. Specific conclusions and recommendations from the report were not immediately detailed in early coverage, but the document is expected to draw significant scrutiny from lawmakers and school administrators.

The release comes as Colorado continues to grapple with school safety legislation, including ongoing debates over the Claire Davis School Safety Act and bills targeting mental health resources for students.

stories.headline: Colorado's right-to-repair movement grows, but new bill could roll back recent protections
stories.summary:
Repair cafés, monthly volunteer-run events where community members can bring broken household items to be fixed for free, have spread to about a dozen locations across Colorado, including a well-established monthly event in Longmont. The events are part of a broader right-to-repair movement that aims to keep consumer goods out of landfills and reduce dependence on manufacturers for repairs.

Colorado was among the first states to pass right-to-repair legislation, beginning with powered wheelchairs in 2022 and expanding to agricultural equipment and digital devices in subsequent years. A law requiring manufacturers of digital equipment to support independent repairs took effect in January 2026.

However, Senate Bill 90, which passed the state Senate 22-13 on April 16 and is now awaiting action in the House, would create exemptions for technology used in critical infrastructure. Consumer groups including CoPIRG and iFixit argue the bill's definition of "critical infrastructure" is broad enough to effectively nullify existing right-to-repair protections, while technology companies including Cisco and IBM support the measure on cybersecurity grounds.

stories.headline: Two teenagers hospitalized after car crashes into second story of church near Colorado Springs
stories.summary:
Two teenagers were taken to the hospital — one with critical injuries — after their vehicle veered off the road and struck the second story of a church in Security-Widefield, a community south of Colorado Springs, late Saturday night. The Colorado State Patrol said the crash occurred around 9:40 p.m. when the car was heading south on Main Street and failed to complete a right turn.

The Colorado State Patrol is investigating the cause of the crash. The church sustained significant structural damage from the impact.

Security-Widefield is an unincorporated community in El Paso County. The identities of the teenagers involved have not been released.

weather.notable: Nearly 7 degrees below the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: Laughter gives us distance. It allows us to step back from an event, deal with it, and then move on.

topStory.headline: Armed man breaches security checkpoint at White House Correspondents' Dinner; Secret Service officer injured
topStory.summary:
An armed man breached a security checkpoint at the White House Correspondents' Association dinner in Washington, D.C., on Saturday evening and exchanged gunfire with law enforcement before being apprehended. The suspect, identified as Cole Tomas Allen, 31, of Torrance, California, was armed with a shotgun, a handgun and multiple knives when he rushed a checkpoint in the lobby of the Washington Hilton at approximately 8:36 p.m. A Secret Service officer was injured in the exchange of gunfire; no other injuries were reported.

President Donald Trump and senior members of his Cabinet and Congress were quickly evacuated from the hotel's ballroom, where the annual dinner was underway. Ahead of the incident, Allen had sent writings to family members displaying anti-Trump sentiment and stating that his targets were administration officials, not hotel guests or employees, according to a senior administration official who spoke to NBC News. Allen's brother notified police in Connecticut after receiving the writings, in which Allen apologized to family and friends but said he did not expect forgiveness.

Allen held a bachelor's degree in mechanical engineering from the California Institute of Technology and a master's degree in computer science from California State University, Dominguez Hills. A former high school teammate described him as a 'borderline genius' and 'super stable,' calling the incident deeply out of character. Officials said Allen had no criminal record and was not previously on the radar of law enforcement in Washington, D.C.

Investigators believe Allen was able to penetrate the outermost layer of security because he was a registered guest of the hotel, which had been closed to the general public since 2 p.m. The approximately 2,300 ticketed dinner guests were required to pass through multiple additional security layers to enter the ballroom, including magnetometers staffed by the Secret Service and the Transportation Security Administration. Secret Service Director Sean Curran and Metropolitan Police Interim Chief Jeffery Carroll both argued Saturday that the incident demonstrated that the multi-layered security system functioned as designed.

The shooting is expected to intensify scrutiny of security protocols surrounding the president and high-profile political events, particularly given the venue's significance — the Washington Hilton was the site of the 1981 assassination attempt on President Ronald Reagan. The incident comes amid a period of heightened concern over politically motivated violence in the United States. Federal charges against Allen are anticipated, and investigators continue to examine the writings he sent to his family in the hours before the attack.
